[...]


655:                END IF


656:             CASE (nnp_cut_tanh)


657:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


658:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


659:                   fc_tmp = 1.0_dp - neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)*sg%inv_cut


660:                   scratch_fc1(j) = hermite_spline_value(spl_tanh, fc_tmp)**3


661:                END DO


662:                !$OMP END DO


663:                IF (sg%ele(1) /= sg%ele(2)) THEN


664:                   !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(static)


665:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


666:                      fc_tmp = 1.0_dp - neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)*sg%inv_cut


667:                      scratch_fc2(k) = hermite_spline_value(spl_tanh, fc_tmp)**3


668:                   END DO


669:                   !$OMP END DO


670:                END IF


671:             END SELECT


672:             ! implicit barrier at end of last !$OMP DO publishes scratch_fc* to all threads


673: 


674:             IF (sg%ele(1) == sg%ele(2)) THEN


675:                !$OMP DO SCHEDULE(dynamic, 4)


676:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


677:                   rvect1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, j, s)


678:                   r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


679:                   DO k = j + 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


680:                      rvect2 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, k, s)


681:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, k, s)


682:                      rvect3 = rvect2 - rvect1


683:                      r3sq = rvect3(1)*rvect3(1) + rvect3(2)*rvect3(2) + rvect3(3)*rvect3(3)


684:                      IF (r3sq < sg%cutoff_sq) THEN


685:                         r3 = SQRT(r3sq)


686:                         CALL nnp_calc_ang(nnp, ind, s, rvect1, rvect2, rvect3, r1, r2, r3, symtmp, &


687:                                           fc1_in=scratch_fc1(j), fc2_in=scratch_fc1(k))


688:                         DO sf = 1, n_symf_s


689:                            local_y(sf) = local_y(sf) + symtmp(sf)


690:                         END DO


691:                      END IF


692:                   END DO


693:                END DO


694:                !$OMP END DO NOWAIT


695:             ELSE


696:                !$OMP DO COLLAPSE(2) SCHEDULE(dynamic, 16)


697:                DO j = 1, neighbor%n_ang1(s)


698:                   DO k = 1, neighbor%n_ang2(s)


699:                      rvect1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(1:3, j, s)


700:                      r1 = neighbor%dist_ang1(4, j, s)


701:                      rvect2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(1:3, k, s)


702:                      r2 = neighbor%dist_ang2(4, k, s)


703:                      rvect3 = rvect2 - rvect1


704:                      r3sq = rvect3(1)*rvect3(1) + rvect3(2)*rvect3(2) + rvect3(3)*rvect3(3)


705:                      IF (r3sq < sg%cutoff_sq) THEN


706:                         r3 = SQRT(r3sq)


707:                         CALL nnp_calc_ang(nnp, ind, s, rvect1, rvect2, rvect3, r1, r2, r3, symtmp, &


708:                                           fc1_in=scratch_fc1(j), fc2_in=scratch_fc2(k))


709:                         DO sf = 1, n_symf_s


710:                            local_y(sf) = local_y(sf) + symtmp(sf)


711:                         END DO


712:                      END IF


713:                   END DO


714:                END DO


715:                !$OMP END DO NOWAIT

732:       CALL nnp_check_extrapolation(nnp, ind)

735:          IF (PRESENT(stress)) THEN

737:                CALL nnp_scale_acsf(nnp, ind, dsymdxyz, stress, active_atoms, n_active)

746:             END IF

751: 


752:       ! Persistent buffer — no release here; freed at program end via SAVE finalisation.


753:       CALL timestop(handle)


754: 


755:    END SUBROUTINE nnp_calc_acsf


756: 


757: ! **************************************************************************************************


758: !> \brief Check if the nnp is extrapolating


759: !> \param nnp ...


760: !> \param ind ...


761: !> \date   2020-10-10


762: !> \author Christoph Schran (christoph.schran@rub.de)


763: ! **************************************************************************************************


764:    SUBROUTINE nnp_check_extrapolation(nnp, ind)


765: 


766:       TYPE(nnp_type), INTENT(INOUT)                      :: nnp


767:       INTEGER, INTENT(IN)                                :: ind


768: 


769:       REAL(KIND=dp), PARAMETER                           :: threshold = 0.0001_dp


770: 


771:       INTEGER                                            :: j


772:       LOGICAL                                            :: extrapolate


773: 


774:       extrapolate = nnp%output_expol


775: 


776:       DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


777:          IF (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - &


778:              nnp%rad(ind)%loc_max(j) > threshold) THEN


779:             extrapolate = .TRUE.


780:          ELSE IF (-nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) + &


781:                   nnp%rad(ind)%loc_min(j) > threshold) THEN


782:             extrapolate = .TRUE.


783:          END IF


784:       END DO


785:       DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


786:          IF (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - &


787:              nnp%ang(ind)%loc_max(j) > threshold) THEN


788:             extrapolate = .TRUE.


789:          ELSE IF (-nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) + &


790:                   nnp%ang(ind)%loc_min(j) > threshold) THEN


791:             extrapolate = .TRUE.


792:          END IF


793:       END DO


794: 


[...]


801: !> \param nnp ...


802: !> \param ind ...


803: !> \param dsymdxyz ...


804: !> \param stress ...


805: !> \date   2020-10-10


806: !> \author Christoph Schran (christoph.schran@rub.de)


807: ! **************************************************************************************************


808:    SUBROUTINE nnp_scale_acsf(nnp, ind, dsymdxyz, stress, active_atoms, n_active)


809: 


810:       TYPE(nnp_type), INTENT(INOUT)                      :: nnp


811:       INTEGER, INTENT(IN)                                :: ind


812:       REAL(KIND=dp), DIMENSION(:, :, :), INTENT(OUT), &


813:          OPTIONAL                                        :: dsymdxyz, stress


814:       INTEGER, DIMENSION(:), OPTIONAL, INTENT(IN)        :: active_atoms


815:       INTEGER, OPTIONAL, INTENT(IN)                      :: n_active


816: 


817:       INTEGER                                            :: idx, j, k, off


818: 


819:       ! Build the input layer (node) array from the centre's symmetry function values.


820:       ! Small loop (n_rad + n_ang elements) — kept serial.  Sub-expressions like


821:       ! (scmax-scmin)/(loc_max-loc_min) are now folded into nnp%*%scale_factor and


822:       ! nnp%*%inv_sigma_factor, filled once at init in nnp_init_model.


823:       IF (nnp%center_acsf) THEN


824:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_rad(ind)


825:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j) = &


826:                (nnp%rad(ind)%y(j) - nnp%rad(ind)%loc_av(j))


827:          END DO


828:          off = nnp%n_rad(ind)


829:          DO j = 1, nnp%n_ang(ind)


830:             nnp%arc(ind)%layer(1)%node(j + off) = &


831:                (nnp%ang(ind)%y(j) - nnp%ang(ind)%loc_av(j))

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 578.00 to 107.80 cycles (5.36x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 15% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • __intel_avx_rep_memcpy: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:710
    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:714


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • RET: 1 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:831
  • VZEROUPPER: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:714
    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:831


Type of elements and instruction set


26 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).


Matching between your function (in the source code) and the binary function


The binary function is composed of 26 FP arithmetical operations:

  • 26: addition or subtraction
The binary function is loading 6384 bytes (798 double precision FP elements).
The binary function is storing 928 bytes (116 double precision FP elements).

Cycles and memory resources usage


Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 578.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 8% of peak load performance is reached (11.04 out of 128.00 bytes loaded per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
  • 2% of peak store performance is reached (1.61 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))

Performance is limited by instruction throughput (loading/decoding program instructions to execution core) (front-end is a bottleneck).

Code clean check


Detected a slowdown caused by scalar integer instructions (typically used for address computation).
By removing them, you can lower the cost of an iteration from 578.00 to 107.80 cycles (5.36x speedup).

Workaround


  • Try to reorganize arrays of structures to structures of arrays
  • Consider to permute loops (see vectorization gain report)
  • To reference allocatable arrays, use "allocatable" instead of "pointer" pointers or qualify them with the "contiguous" attribute (Fortran 2008)
  • For structures, limit to one indirection. For example, use a_b%c instead of a%b%c with a_b set to a%b before this function


Vectorization


Your function is probably not vectorized.
Only 15% of vector register length is used (average across all SSE/AVX instructions).


Details


Store and arithmetical SSE/AVX instructions are used in scalar version (process only one data element in vector registers).


Workaround


Read the "512-bits vectorization" report at "Potential" confidence level.

Execution units bottlenecks


Found no such bottlenecks but see expert reports for more complex bottlenecks.


  


512-bits vectorization


On some x86 processors supporting 512-bits vectorization, compilers are often too conservative and limit vectorization to 256 bits. Performance can then be improved by enforcing 512-bits vectorization, especially with many vectorized and high trip count loops. 512-bits vectorization performance overhead (compared to 256-bits) is generally lower on newer processors.


Workaround


Recompile with -qopt-zmm-usage=high


  


CALL instructions


Detected function call instructions.


Details


Calling (and then returning from) a function prevents many compiler optimizations (like vectorization), breaks control flow (which reduces pipeline performance) and executes extra instructions to save/restore the registers used inside it, which is very expensive (dozens of cycles). Consider to inline small functions.

  • __intel_avx_rep_memcpy: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:710
    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:714


Complex instructions


Detected COMPLEX INSTRUCTIONS.


Details


These instructions generate more than one micro-operation and only one of them can be decoded during a cycle and the extra micro-operations increase pressure on execution units.

  • RET: 1 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:831
  • VZEROUPPER: 2 occurrences►

    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:714
    • /home/crm98/cp2k_optimized/build/src/nnp_acsf.F:831

26 SSE or AVX instructions are processing arithmetic or math operations on double precision FP elements in scalar mode (one at a time).

Assuming all data fit into the L1 cache, each call to the function takes 578.00 cycles. At this rate:

  • 2% of peak store performance is reached (1.61 out of 64.00 bytes stored per cycle (GB/s @ 1GHz))
